name: Arianrhod
description: Arianrhod, whose name translates to "Silver Wheel" or "Silver Circle," is a prominent figure in Welsh mythology, particularly within the Mabinogion. She is often associated with the moon, stars, and celestial realms, embodying themes of sovereignty, fate, and cyclical time. As a goddess of the night sky, Arianrhod’s silver wheel symbolizes the turning of the year, the cycles of life and death, and the ever-changing nature of the cosmos. Her fortress, Caer Arianrhod, located in the constellation Corona Borealis according to some interpretations, represents a celestial haven and a place of power. Arianrhod's role in the Fourth Branch of the Mabinogi, specifically her interactions with the magician Gwydion and her unwilling motherhood of Lleu Llaw Gyffes, highlight her complex character. She embodies the tension between free will and destiny, and the consequences of unchecked power. Her curses and geasa placed upon Lleu are pivotal to his narrative, demonstrating her influence over his fate and the unfolding of events in the mythological cycle. Arianrhod's story serves as a cautionary tale about the abuse of power and the enduring strength of fate.
subtitle: Silver Wheel

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Lleu Llaw Gyffes
keyMyths.description: Arianrhod is tricked by Gwydion into proving her virginity through a magical test. During this test, she gives birth to Dylan Eil Ton and Lleu Llaw Gyffes, though she denies Lleu his name and arms, placing geasa upon him that only she can lift. Gwydion circumvents these geasa through cunning and trickery, demonstrating the conflict between Arianrhod's will and the power of magic.

keyMyths.title: Denying Name and Arms
keyMyths.description: Arianrhod, angered by Gwydion's trickery and her forced motherhood, refuses to give Lleu a name or weapons. This act of denial is a significant blow to Lleu's identity and status, preventing him from achieving his full potential as a hero. Gwydion once again intervenes, using illusion to trick Arianrhod into bestowing both name and arms upon Lleu, highlighting her vulnerability to magical manipulation.

keyMyths.title: The Geas on Lleu
keyMyths.description: Arianrhod places a geas on Lleu that he will never marry a woman of this earth. This curse is a significant obstacle in Lleu's life, preventing him from finding happiness and fulfillment. Gwydion and Math, through their combined magical abilities, create a wife for Lleu from flowers, named Blodeuwedd, to circumvent Arianrhod's curse, leading to further tragedy and demonstrating the far-reaching consequences of her actions.

sources.author: Sioned Davies

sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Celtic Religion
sources.description: Explores the symbolism and religious significance of Celtic deities, including potential interpretations of Arianrhod's role.

sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Celtic mythology, providing historical and cultural context for understanding Arianrhod and her place within the pantheon.
